accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3 states service credits are 'Customer's sole and exclusive remedies' + Section 4.4(b) requires Customer to 'pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach + Section 4.3 gives Customer only 60-day cure window for Lattice breach + Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from four corners
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer requires: (1) Exhibit A attached before execution with uptime ≥99.5% and credit cap ≤25% monthly fees; (2) right to terminate for cause without remainder-of-term payment if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months; (3) mutual 30-day cure periods in Section 4.3; (4) clarification that liability cap in Section 8.6 applies to aggregate SLA credit claims.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Before execution, we need the Service Level Agreement attached and reviewed. The interaction of the missing SLA, sole-remedy language, full-term payment obligation on early exit, and asymmetric cure periods creates uncertainty around performance standards and remedies. We'd like to see the SLA terms and align termination rights with service-level failures.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Performance standards are undefined, the sole remedy for failures is unknown, early exit for non-breach costs 100% of remaining fees, and exit for breach requires a 60-day wait. These provisions compound to eliminate meaningful recourse if service quality proves inadequate.
accepted_findings.position: Before execution: (1) Exhibit A must be attached with commercially reasonable SLA terms. (2) Add termination-for-cause right if SLA credits exceed materiality threshold (e.g., 25% monthly fees for 2 consecutive months) with no remainder-of-term payment obligation. (3) Mutual 30-day cure periods. (4) Clarify liability-cap application to SLA credits. (5) If Customer must terminate for non-breach reasons, cap payment at 50% of remainder or 3 months' fees, whichever is less.
accepted_findings.fallback: (1) Exhibit A provided with 5-day review period and right to negotiate uptime threshold and credit cap. (2) Termination right if SLA credits exceed 50% monthly fees for 2 consecutive months, with 75% remainder-of-term payment. (3) Accept 60/30 cure asymmetry if SLA termination right is granted. (4) Accept liability cap subsumes SLA credits if cap is increased to 2x fees for SLA-related claims.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Signing this Agreement with Exhibit A missing or with no termination right for repeated SLA failures combined with 100% remainder-of-term payment for non-breach termination.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer requires: (1) Exhibit A attached before execution with uptime ≥99.5% and credit cap ≤25% monthly fees; (2) right to terminate for cause without remainder-of-term payment if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months; (3) mutual 30-day cure periods in Section 4.3; (4) clarification that liability cap in Section 8.6 applies to aggregate SLA credit claims.

accepted_findings.category: Exhibit A not provided — SLA obligations unknown
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: Lattice will provide the Subscription Services in substantial conformance with the service-level commitments set forth in the Service Level Schedule attached as Exhibit A.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Service Level Agreement referenced throughout the contract has not been provided. We cannot accept service-level obligations without reviewing the actual uptime commitments, credit formulas, exclusions, and measurement methodologies. We request that Exhibit A be attached and made available for review and negotiation before execution.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without the SLA document, we have no enforceable performance standard and cannot assess whether the service credits provide adequate remedies. The contract makes SLA credits the sole remedy for performance failures, but the credits themselves are completely undefined.
accepted_findings.position: Require Exhibit A to be attached and negotiated before execution. Exhibit A must include uptime ≥99.5%, tiered response/resolution SLAs, credit formula with cap not to exceed 25% of monthly fees, and objective exclusions.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept Exhibit A as vendor's standard form but require at least five business days to review before execution and a right to propose amendments to uptime threshold and credit cap.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Signing this Agreement without Exhibit A attached.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose equal cure periods for both parties. The current structure gives Lattice twice the time to remedy a breach, which delays our ability to respond to material service failures. Mutual 30-day cure periods are standard in balanced commercial agreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods extend the time Customer must wait before terminating for Lattice's breach, requiring continued payment and acceptance of substandard service during the extended cure window.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 30-day cure periods for material breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 45-day cure periods for material breach.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

accepted_findings.source_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The 60-day notice period requires advance planning that may not align with our budget and planning cycles. A 30-day notice period is more consistent with standard SaaS renewal practices and reduces the risk of inadvertent renewal.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Missing a 60-day notice window by even a few days locks Customer into a full additional one-year term at fees that may no longer be competitive or aligned with business needs.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day non-renewal notice period.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day non-renewal notice period.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Unless otherwise specified in an Order Form, upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods (each, a "Renewal Term") unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.

accepted_findings.source_text: any dispute, claim, or controversy arising out of or relating to this Agreement, including the breach, termination, enforcement, interpretation, or validity thereof, or the determination of the scope or applicability of this agreement to arbitrate, will be resolved exclusively by final and binding arbitration administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules. The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: any dispute, claim, or controversy arising out of or relating to this Agreement, including the breach, termination, enforcement, interpretation, or validity thereof, or the determination of the scope or applicability of this agreement to arbitrate, will be resolved exclusively by final and binding arbitration administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules. The arbitration will be conducted in a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or if the Parties cannot agree, at a location designated by the arbitrator that is reasonably convenient to both Parties, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring arbitration exclusively in San Francisco imposes travel and counsel costs on us for any dispute. Industry practice for balanced provisions is to use a neutral location or a forum reasonably convenient to both parties.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Mandatory San Francisco arbitration requires us to retain California counsel and travel to California for hearings and proceedings. For a mid-market customer, this can add significant incremental costs to any dispute, effectively reducing the economic viability of pursuing claims.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state or mutually agreed neutral location.
accepted_findings.fallback: Arbitration in a location designated by the arbitrator that is reasonably convenient to both Parties, considering factors such as location of witnesses, documents, and parties' principal places of business.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: any dispute, claim, or controversy arising out of or relating to this Agreement, including the breach, termination, enforcement, interpretation, or validity thereof, or the determination of the scope or applicability of this agreement to arbitrate, will be resolved exclusively by final and binding arbitration administered by the American Arbitration Association under its Commercial Arbitration Rules. The arbitration will be conducted in a location mutually agreed by the Parties, or if the Parties cannot agree, at a location designated by the arbitrator that is reasonably convenient to both Parties, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs: '(D) DAMAGES ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO A SECURITY INCIDENT (AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3) INVOLVING CUSTOMER DATA, UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE, WHICHEVER IS GREATER,'
accepted_findings.external_comment: We require a super-cap for Security Incidents involving our data. Given the notification obligations and the nature of the data being processed, exposure for data breaches should not be subject to the general cap. A 3x super-cap or insurance-limit coverage is standard in our SaaS agreements for cyber incidents.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a data-breach super-cap, recovery for a material Security Incident involving our data would be capped at 12 months' subscription fees, which could be far lower than actual damages in a material breach. We face regulatory exposure and third-party claims that can easily exceed this amount.
accepted_findings.position: Super-cap of 3x fees or insurance limits, whichever is greater, for Security Incidents involving Customer Data.
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap of 2x fees for Security Incidents involving Customer Data, or insurance limits if disclosed and reasonable.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs: '(D) DAMAGES ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO A SECURITY INCIDENT (AS DEFINED IN SECTION 5.3) INVOLVING CUSTOMER DATA, UP TO A MAXIMUM OF THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT, OR THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE, WHICHEVER IS GREATER,'

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 6.2A — Customer-Specific Deliverables. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other work product that Lattice creates specifically for Customer pursuant to an Order Form or SOW and that has no independent utility to Lattice apart from Customer's specific use case ('Customer-Specific Deliverables') shall be owned by Customer. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in and to Customer-Specific Deliverables upon Customer's payment in full of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains ownership of all general-purpose tools, methodologies, algorithms, and pre-existing materials as set forth in Section 2.5.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request clarification that customer-specific outputs — such as reports, dashboards, or analytics generated uniquely for our use case — are owned by us upon payment. This does not affect Lattice's retention of its platform, tools, or methodologies.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: We will pay Professional Services fees for implementation and potentially custom analytics. Without clarity on deliverable ownership, we risk paying for work product we cannot retain, repurpose, or port to a successor vendor upon termination.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns all Customer-Specific Deliverables (reports, dashboards, analytics) created for Customer. Lattice retains platform, tools, and methodologies.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable, fully paid-up license to Customer-Specific Deliverables, with right to modify and create derivative works for Customer's internal use.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 6.2A — Customer-Specific Deliverables. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other work product that Lattice creates specifically for Customer pursuant to an Order Form or SOW and that has no independent utility to Lattice apart from Customer's specific use case ('Customer-Specific Deliverables') shall be owned by Customer. Lattice hereby assigns to Customer all right, title, and interest in and to Customer-Specific Deliverables upon Customer's payment in full of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains ownership of all general-purpose tools, methodologies, algorithms, and pre-existing materials as set forth in Section 2.5.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, but only with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request additional-insured status on the E&O policy to ensure we have direct coverage for claims arising from errors or omissions in service delivery. This protection is standard in SaaS agreements where the customer faces potential third-party exposure from the provider's acts.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status, we must rely on Lattice's indemnity obligation and may face gaps if Lattice's carrier denies coverage or if Lattice becomes insolvent. Direct coverage as additional insured closes this gap for E&O claims.
accepted_findings.position: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, but only with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy to the extent available under Lattice's policy form, with respect to claims arising from Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, but only with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services to Customer under this Agreement.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) requires 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' + Section 8.6 liability cap at 12 months' fees + finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes data-breach super-cap of '3x fees or insurance limits, whichever is greater' + Section 5.3 requires Security Incident notification within 72 hours + no requirement that Customer be loss payee or that cyber coverage be dedicated standalone policy
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Require: (1) Lattice maintain dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance with $5M limits (not merely E&O with cyber endorsement). (2) Certificate of insurance provided annually showing cyber coverage with no material sublimits or exclusions for first-party data restoration or third-party regulatory defense. (3) Customer named as loss payee on cyber policy for claims arising from Security Incidents. (4) Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 tied explicitly to verified insurance limits, not merely 'whichever is greater' based on undisclosed policy terms.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The proposed data-breach super-cap references insurance limits, but the current language does not mandate dedicated cyber insurance. If Lattice relies solely on E&O coverage with limited cyber endorsements, we may face uninsured exposure in a Security Incident. We request clarification that Lattice maintains standalone cyber liability insurance and that we are named as loss payee for cyber claims.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If the super-cap references insurance limits but those limits are ambiguous or inaccessible to us, the super-cap is effectively illusory. In a material Security Incident, we could face exposure exceeding the 12-month general cap but be unable to collect under the super-cap.
accepted_findings.position: Require dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance at $5M with no material sublimits. Customer named as loss payee. Certificate provided annually. Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 set at 3x fees OR verified cyber insurance limits shown on annual certificate, whichever is greater.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept E&O with cyber endorsement if Lattice provides annual certificate evidencing that cyber sublimits are at least $5M and that first-party data restoration and third-party regulatory defense are covered without material exclusions. Customer named as additional insured (not loss payee) on E&O policy. Super-cap set at 2x fees or insurance limits.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Require: (1) Lattice maintain dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance with $5M limits (not merely E&O with cyber endorsement). (2) Certificate of insurance provided annually showing cyber coverage with no material sublimits or exclusions for first-party data restoration or third-party regulatory defense. (3) Customer named as loss payee on cyber policy for claims arising from Security Incidents. (4) Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 tied explicitly to verified insurance limits, not merely 'whichever is greater' based on undisclosed policy terms.

accepted_findings.source_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose adjusting the late-payment interest rate to 1% per month, which is more typical for customer-side SaaS agreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 1.5% monthly (18% APR) is above market for buyer-side SaaS terms. The incremental 0.5% monthly compounds to material cost over extended late-payment scenarios.
accepted_findings.position: 1% per month maximum.
accepted_findings.fallback: 1.25% per month if vendor insists on rate above 1%.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT,'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap should not apply to gross negligence or willful misconduct by either party. This is a standard carve-out in commercial agreements to preserve accountability for egregious conduct. The mutual application ensures neither party benefits from its own bad acts.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Capping liability for gross negligence or willful misconduct eliminates deterrence and effectively licenses reckless or intentional harmful conduct. Even deliberate harm would be subject to the 12-month cap, which is commercially unreasonable.
accepted_findings.position: Uncapped liability for gross negligence and willful misconduct by either party.
accepted_findings.fallback: Uncapped liability for willful misconduct only; gross negligence subject to a 3x super-cap.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 8.6 carve-outs: '(E) EITHER PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE OR WILLFUL MISCONDUCT,'

accepted_findings.source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request a five-year post-termination confidentiality tail for non-trade-secret information. Commercial and operational information disclosed during the relationship will retain competitive sensitivity beyond a three-year horizon.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: We will disclose operational, strategic, and commercial information to Lattice during implementation and ongoing use. A three-year tail does not adequately protect this information against competitive use or disclosure during its natural sensitivity lifecycle.
accepted_findings.position: Five (5) years post-termination for non-trade-secret Confidential Information; indefinite for trade secrets.
accepted_findings.fallback: Four (4) years post-termination if Lattice demonstrates burden.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A), Customer shall pay Fees accrued through the effective date of termination
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring full remaining fees even when we terminate for non-breach reasons creates a penalty that exceeds actual damages. We propose limiting our payment obligation to fees earned through termination, or alternatively, to a reasonable breakage percentage.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If we terminate after six months of a 12-month term for reasons other than material breach—including repeated SLA failures or changed business needs—we owe 100% of the remaining six months' fees with no corresponding service delivery. This is effectively a liquidated-damages clause without a reasonableness cap.
accepted_findings.position: Customer pays only fees accrued through the effective termination date if Customer terminates for non-breach reasons.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer pays fees accrued through termination plus 25% of fees for the remainder of the term, capped at three months of fees, if Customer terminates for non-breach reasons.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A), Customer shall pay Fees accrued through the effective date of termination

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase shall not exceed five percent (5%) annually or the increase in the U.S. Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) over the preceding twelve-month period, whichever is greater. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose capping annual fee increases at the greater of 5% or CPI-U, which protects both parties from volatility while allowing reasonable inflation adjustments.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped price escalation creates unbounded renewal-cost exposure. Lattice may unilaterally increase fees by any amount with 60 days' notice, eliminating our ability to budget multi-year SaaS spend or compare renewal pricing to market alternatives.
accepted_findings.position: Cap annual increases at greater of 5% or CPI-U.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap at greater of 7.5% or CPI-U; or cap at 10% flat with no CPI tie.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase shall not exceed five percent (5%) annually or the increase in the U.S. Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) over the preceding twelve-month period, whichever is greater. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience by Customer. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form for any reason or no reason upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice. In the event of such termination, Customer will pay Lattice (a) all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and (b) fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement does not provide us with any termination right other than for material breach. If our business needs change, we're locked in for the full term with no exit option. We propose a termination-for-convenience right with a reasonable breakage fee.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Absence of termination for convenience means we cannot exit even if the Services become redundant due to acquisition of a competing product, downsizing, or strategic shift. Given the auto-renewal mechanics, we could be bound for multi-year commitments with no remedy.
accepted_findings.position: Customer termination for convenience with 90 days' notice and 50% breakage fee for remainder of term.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer termination for convenience with 90 days' notice and 75% breakage fee for remainder of term, or termination for convenience after the first 12 months with 100% payment for the first year if earlier.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience by Customer. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form for any reason or no reason upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice. In the event of such termination, Customer will pay Lattice (a) all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and (b) fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term.

accepted_findings.category: Acceptance criteria discretionary for Professional Services
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.5: Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables (if any), fees, milestones, and any acceptance criteria.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 2.5: Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables, fees, milestones, and objective acceptance criteria. If no acceptance criteria are specified, deliverables shall be deemed accepted if Customer does not provide written notice of material non-conformance within ten (10) business days of delivery.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose making acceptance criteria mandatory for all Professional Services deliverables and establishing a deemed-acceptance period to avoid disputes. Clear acceptance criteria protect both parties by establishing objective performance standards.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without mandatory acceptance criteria, we have no objective recourse if deliverables are incomplete or non-conforming, and Lattice has no certainty about when its obligations are discharged. In a services engagement where fees are substantial, this ambiguity invites disputes.
accepted_findings.position: Make acceptance criteria mandatory; add deemed-acceptance period of 10 business days with objective non-conformance standard.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept optional acceptance criteria but add deemed-acceptance period of 15 business days for any deliverable where acceptance criteria are specified.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 2.5: Each SOW will identify the scope of work, deliverables, fees, milestones, and objective acceptance criteria. If no acceptance criteria are specified, deliverables shall be deemed accepted if Customer does not provide written notice of material non-conformance within ten (10) business days of delivery.

accepted_findings.category: Lattice modification rights may degrade service during term
accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.4: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Section 2.4: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality or performance of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term. Lattice will provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' advance written notice of any modification that may reasonably be expected to adversely affect Customer's use of the Subscription Services.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose adding 'or performance' to the non-diminishment standard and requiring advance notice of material changes. The current language protects 'core functionality' but does not address performance degradation. Notice of material changes allows us to assess impact and plan accordingly.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The 'core functionality' standard is subjective and does not prevent Lattice from degrading SLA-level performance as long as the feature set remains. Advance notice of adverse changes provides time to mitigate or renegotiate.
accepted_findings.position: Add 'or performance' and 30-day advance-notice requirement as drafted above.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add 'or performance' language; accept omission of advance-notice requirement.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Section 2.4: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality or performance of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term. Lattice will provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' advance written notice of any modification that may reasonably be expected to adversely affect Customer's use of the Subscription Services.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material adverse change to any insurance policy required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request advance notice of policy cancellation or material change so we can evaluate our risk posture and exercise contractual remedies if coverage lapses. Thirty days is standard notice for insurance-certificate holders.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice's E&O or CGL policy is canceled mid-term without our knowledge, we are exposed to uninsured counterparty risk during the gap. Advance notice allows us to suspend Services or terminate if coverage lapses.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material adverse change to any insurance policy required under Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice shall use commercially reasonable efforts to provide Customer with advance notice of any cancellation or non-renewal of required insurance policies, to the extent Lattice receives such notice from its carrier.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any cancellation, non-renewal, or material adverse change to any insurance policy required under Section 9.2.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance required under Section 9.2(b) (errors and omissions / professional liability) shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or Lattice shall procure tail coverage for such three-year period.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Claims arising from errors or omissions during the Subscription Term may not be discovered or asserted until after termination. Tail coverage for E&O ensures we retain the benefit of insurance for claims with delayed discovery. Three years is a standard tail period.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: E&O claims often emerge months or years after the underlying act. If Lattice terminates its E&O policy at contract end with no tail, we have no insurance backstop for late-discovered claims arising from Lattice's work during the term.
accepted_findings.position: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance required under Section 9.2(b) (errors and omissions / professional liability) shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or Lattice shall procure tail coverage for such three-year period.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice's obligation to maintain errors and omissions / professional liability insurance shall survive termination for one (1) year, or Lattice shall procure tail coverage for such one-year period.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's obligation to maintain the insurance required under Section 9.2(b) (errors and omissions / professional liability) shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of three (3) years, or Lattice shall procure tail coverage for such three-year period.

accepted_findings.source_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services or the business of Lattice ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback for any purpose, without any obligation or compensation to Customer.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users voluntarily provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, or ideas regarding the Subscription Services ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback solely for purposes of improving the Subscription Services and developing Lattice's products and services, without any obligation or compensation to Customer. Feedback does not include Customer Data, Customer-Specific Deliverables, or Customer's business processes, strategies, or confidential information.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request that the Feedback grant be scoped to suggestions about the platform itself, and clarified to exclude our data, deliverables created for us, and our proprietary business information. This ensures we do not inadvertently assign ownership of our own confidential methods or data.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The current Feedback clause sweeps in 'any... feedback regarding... the business of Lattice' without limitation. We may share detailed information about our own business processes, data models, or operational strategies during implementation and support interactions. An unqualified assignment risks transferring our proprietary methods.
accepted_findings.position: Feedback limited to suggestions about the Subscription Services platform. Explicitly excludes Customer Data, Customer-Specific Deliverables, and Customer's proprietary business processes.
accepted_findings.fallback: Scope Feedback to the Subscription Services (remove 'business of Lattice') and add exclusion for Customer Confidential Information.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users voluntarily provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, or ideas regarding the Subscription Services ('Feedback'), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback solely for purposes of improving the Subscription Services and developing Lattice's products and services, without any obligation or compensation to Customer. Feedback does not include Customer Data, Customer-Specific Deliverables, or Customer's business processes, strategies, or confidential information.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, a "material breach" means a breach that (a) substantially deprives the non-breaching Party of the benefit it reasonably expected to receive under this Agreement, and (b) cannot be reasonably remedied within the applicable cure period. Material breach includes, without limitation: (i) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due (by Customer); (ii) repeated failure to meet the Service Level Commitments set forth in Exhibit A (by Lattice); (iii) breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions) or Section 7 (Confidentiality) that causes demonstrable harm; or (iv) any breach of a representation or warranty in Section 8.1.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement uses "materially breaches" as the termination standard but does not define materiality. Without a definition, the Parties may have divergent interpretations of what constitutes a terminating event, leading to disputes over whether a breach justifies termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Ambiguity in the material-breach standard creates risk: if we seek to terminate for Lattice's breach, Lattice may argue the breach was not material, forcing us into arbitration to prove termination was proper—during which time we may owe continued fees. A defined standard reduces this risk.
accepted_findings.position: Insert definition of material breach with specific examples tied to this Agreement's core obligations.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add carve-out clarifying that repeated SLA failures (as defined in Exhibit A) and uncured payment defaults (30+ days overdue) are per se material breaches.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: For purposes of this Agreement, a "material breach" means a breach that (a) substantially deprives the non-breaching Party of the benefit it reasonably expected to receive under this Agreement, and (b) cannot be reasonably remedied within the applicable cure period. Material breach includes, without limitation: (i) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due (by Customer); (ii) repeated failure to meet the Service Level Commitments set forth in Exhibit A (by Lattice); (iii) breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions) or Section 7 (Confidentiality) that causes demonstrable harm; or (iv) any breach of a representation or warranty in Section 8.1.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Actions seeking equitable relief (including injunctive relief or specific performance) may be brought in any state or federal court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Delaware.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Section 10.2 exempts equitable relief from arbitration but does not specify where such actions may be brought. Without an express venue provision, either party could file in any jurisdiction with personal jurisdiction over the defendant, leading to forum uncertainty.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Equitable relief is time-sensitive. Ambiguity about venue for such actions can lead to jurisdictional disputes that delay relief. Clarifying venue in a neutral or agreed jurisdiction reduces this friction.
accepted_findings.position: Venue for equitable relief in Delaware state or federal courts.
accepted_findings.fallback: Venue for equitable relief in courts of competent jurisdiction where the defendant resides or where the cause of action arose.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Actions seeking equitable relief (including injunctive relief or specific performance) may be brought in any state or federal court of competent jurisdiction in the State of Delaware.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Invoice Disputes. Customer may withhold payment of any disputed invoice amount by providing written notice to Lattice within fifteen (15) days of receipt of such invoice, specifying in reasonable detail the basis for the dispute. Customer shall pay all undisputed amounts in accordance with Section 3.2. The Parties shall work in good faith to resolve any invoice dispute within thirty (30) days of Customer's notice. During the pendency of a good-faith dispute, Lattice shall not suspend the Subscription Services or assess late fees on the disputed amount.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to include a standard invoice-dispute mechanism that allows us to withhold disputed amounts while paying undisputed portions on time, and that suspends late fees and service-suspension rights during good-faith disputes.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a dispute-withholding mechanism, late fees and suspension rights apply even to amounts we dispute in good faith. This eliminates our leverage to resolve billing errors or service-quality issues during the term.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold specific disputed amounts; notice within 15 days; undisputed amounts paid on standard terms; no late fees or suspension during good-faith dispute.
accepted_findings.fallback: Notice within 10 days; 20-day resolution period; suspension requires 10 additional days' notice after dispute resolution.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Invoice Disputes. Customer may withhold payment of any disputed invoice amount by providing written notice to Lattice within fifteen (15) days of receipt of such invoice, specifying in reasonable detail the basis for the dispute. Customer shall pay all undisputed amounts in accordance with Section 3.2. The Parties shall work in good faith to resolve any invoice dispute within thirty (30) days of Customer's notice. During the pendency of a good-faith dispute, Lattice shall not suspend the Subscription Services or assess late fees on the disputed amount.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-005 proposes to allow Customer to terminate without remainder-of-term payment if Customer terminates 'for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A)'. However, Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from the four corners. The carve-out references a defined term that does not exist in the contract. Either: (1) Exhibit A must be attached and must include a definition of 'Service Level Failure' that triggers the payment-relief carve-out, OR (2) the carve-out in termination-remedies-analyst-005 must be redrafted to reference a different, existing standard (e.g., 'if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months' as proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001).
accepted_findings.external_comment: The proposed edit in termination-remedies-analyst-005 creates a dependency on a missing Exhibit A definition. We need to either attach Exhibit A with the 'Service Level Failure' definition, or tie the payment-relief carve-out to the SLA-based termination right proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001 (which uses a quantified credit-threshold standard). Without this alignment, the carve-out is unenforceable.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If we accept termination-remedies-analyst-005 as drafted but Exhibit A remains missing or does not define 'Service Level Failure,' the payment-relief carve-out is meaningless—Lattice will argue the term is undefined and the general rule (100% remainder payment) applies. This leaves us with no payment relief for SLA-driven termination despite the accepted edit's intent.
accepted_findings.position: Before execution: Exhibit A must define 'Service Level Failure' in a manner consistent with the SLA-based termination right proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001. Alternatively, redraft termination-remedies-analyst-005 to reference the quantified SLA credit threshold from critical-issues-auditor-001 rather than the undefined Exhibit A term.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept termination-remedies-analyst-005 as drafted only if Exhibit A is attached with 'Service Level Failure' defined as SLA credits exceeding 50% of monthly fees for two consecutive months.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-005 proposes to allow Customer to terminate without remainder-of-term payment if Customer terminates 'for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A)'. However, Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from the four corners. The carve-out references a defined term that does not exist in the contract. Either: (1) Exhibit A must be attached and must include a definition of 'Service Level Failure' that triggers the payment-relief carve-out, OR (2) the carve-out in termination-remedies-analyst-005 must be redrafted to reference a different, existing standard (e.g., 'if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months' as proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001).

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Accepted findings risk-allocation-analyst-001 (data-breach super-cap) and risk-allocation-analyst-002 (gross negligence / willful misconduct carve-out) add new carve-outs (D) and (E) to Section 8.6. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes to clarify that the data-breach super-cap references 'verified insurance limits shown on annual certificate.' These three edits must be reconciled into a single amended Section 8.6 that enumerates all carve-outs (A) through (E) in sequence and ties the insurance-limit language to the certificate-of-insurance requirement in Section 9.2.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Three separate findings propose additions to the liability-cap carve-outs. We need to consolidate these into a single coherent Section 8.6 amendment that: (1) lists all five carve-outs (A-E), (2) defines the data-breach super-cap with reference to verified insurance limits from the annual certificate required under Section 9.2, and (3) does not create ambiguity about the interaction between the super-cap and the insurance-verification requirement.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If we accept all three findings as separate markup items without consolidation, the final redline will have overlapping or conflicting edits to Section 8.6. The super-cap language must explicitly tie to the certificate-of-insurance timing and content requirements, or the 'insurance limits, whichever is greater' standard becomes unverifiable.
accepted_findings.position: Consolidate risk-allocation-analyst-001, risk-allocation-analyst-002, and critical-issues-auditor-002 into a single amended Section 8.6 that lists carve-outs (A) through (E) and ties the data-breach super-cap to the insurance-certificate verification requirement added in critical-issues-auditor-002.
accepted_findings.fallback: Accept the three findings as separate edits but require legal to reconcile them into a single coherent Section 8.6 before presenting the redline to counterparty.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Accepted findings risk-allocation-analyst-001 (data-breach super-cap) and risk-allocation-analyst-002 (gross negligence / willful misconduct carve-out) add new carve-outs (D) and (E) to Section 8.6. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes to clarify that the data-breach super-cap references 'verified insurance limits shown on annual certificate.' These three edits must be reconciled into a single amended Section 8.6 that enumerates all carve-outs (A) through (E) in sequence and ties the insurance-limit language to the certificate-of-insurance requirement in Section 9.2.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 6.1: 'Lattice and its licensors own and shall retain all right, title, and interest in and to the Subscription Services, the Documentation, and any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on any of the foregoing, including all intellectual property rights therein.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Accepted finding protective-provisions-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 6.2A stating 'Customer owns Customer-Specific Deliverables' (defined as reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics created specifically for Customer with no independent utility to Lattice). However, Section 6.1 states that Lattice retains ownership of 'any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on' the Subscription Services. If a Customer-Specific Deliverable (e.g., a custom dashboard) is implemented as a configuration or modification 'to or based on' the Subscription Services platform, Section 6.1's broad retention language could be read to override Section 6.2A's assignment. The two sections must be reconciled with a carve-out in Section 6.1 or a clarification in Section 6.2A that Customer-Specific Deliverables are 'notwithstanding Section 6.1' owned by Customer.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The proposed Customer-Specific Deliverables ownership provision must explicitly override the broad retention language in Section 6.1. Without this clarification, Lattice could argue that a custom report or dashboard built 'based on' the platform is an 'improvement' or 'derivative work' under Section 6.1 and therefore remains Lattice's property despite the Section 6.2A assignment.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Section 6.1 is not explicitly carved out, the Customer-Specific Deliverables assignment in Section 6.2A is ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. In litigation, Lattice would argue the 'improvements and derivative works' retention language in Section 6.1 controls, leaving us without ownership of work product we paid Professional Services fees to create.
accepted_findings.position: Amend Section 6.2A (protective-provisions-analyst-002) to begin: 'Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other work product that Lattice creates specifically for Customer...' to make clear the assignment overrides the general retention language.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add to Section 6.1: 'except as provided in Section 6.2A with respect to Customer-Specific Deliverables.'
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Accepted finding protective-provisions-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 6.2A stating 'Customer owns Customer-Specific Deliverables' (defined as reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics created specifically for Customer with no independent utility to Lattice). However, Section 6.1 states that Lattice retains ownership of 'any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on' the Subscription Services. If a Customer-Specific Deliverable (e.g., a custom dashboard) is implemented as a configuration or modification 'to or based on' the Subscription Services platform, Section 6.1's broad retention language could be read to override Section 6.2A's assignment. The two sections must be reconciled with a carve-out in Section 6.1 or a clarification in Section 6.2A that Customer-Specific Deliverables are 'notwithstanding Section 6.1' owned by Customer.

rejected_findings.rejection_reason: DEDUPLICATION: This finding is a restatement and aggregation of termination-remedies-analyst-001 (cure asymmetry), termination-remedies-analyst-004 (arbitration location), termination-remedies-analyst-007 (material breach definition), termination-remedies-analyst-003 (termination for convenience), and termination-remedies-analyst-005 (post-termination payment). The critical-issues-auditor correctly identifies the cross-section interaction, but the constituent findings are already accepted individually and address each component of the hazard. Including this meta-finding would duplicate remedies already enumerated in the accepted findings.

DEDUPLICATION: This finding (SLA sole-remedy / liability cap coherence) is subsumed by critical-issues-auditor-001, which addresses the same cross-section hazard (SLA sole-remedy, missing Exhibit A, cure asymmetry, post-termination payment) in a more comprehensive fashion. Accepting both would create redundant markup on the same underlying issue.

DEDUPLICATION: This finding (insurance coverage mismatch / ambiguity) is subsumed by critical-issues-auditor-002, which addresses the same underlying issue (E&O 'may include cyber' language, super-cap enforcement, loss-payee status) in the context of the broader cross-section hazard. Accepting both would duplicate the same request for clarification on dedicated cyber insurance and loss-payee status.

PROPORTIONALITY: The tension between Section 8.2 warranty disclaimers and Section 2.3 SLA obligations / Section 5.2 security obligations is a standard 'general disclaimer vs. specific warranty' structure. Delaware law resolves this in favor of specific warranties controlling. While the specialist correctly notes the tension, the materiality_rationale does not identify concrete harm: Lattice cannot successfully invoke the broad disclaimer to negate express SLA or security commitments, as the specific provisions control. This is a technical drafting note, not a material risk requiring markup.

PROPORTIONALITY: The absence of customer-caused delay relief for Professional Services is correctly identified but rated as 'minor' by the specialist. In a customer_side role, this provision protects the vendor (Lattice), not the customer. The materiality_rationale acknowledges this is 'low-priority' and 'minor.' Given the deal posture (their_paper_high_leverage) and customer role, spending negotiation capital on provisions that protect the vendor is inconsistent with playbook priorities. Rejected as nit-pick.

PROPORTIONALITY: Set-off rights for undisputed amounts are a convenience provision but not material in this context. The materiality_rationale states this is 'not material on most deals' and creates friction only 'when service credits or refunds accumulate.' The specialist does not identify a concrete scenario in this contract where lack of set-off creates quantifiable harm. Rejected as nit-pick.

PROPORTIONALITY: Publicity and marketing rights are correctly identified as absent, but the materiality_rationale reduces to 'reputational risk' and 'reveal competitive strategies' without quantifying harm. The specialist rates this as 'minor' and acknowledges the 'risk is moderate in a high-leverage posture but material enough to raise.' This does not meet the materiality threshold: the three-question gate requires naming concrete harm, not hypothetical concerns. Rejected as nit-pick.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms to our standard accounts-payable cycle of Net-30, which is market-standard for SaaS subscriptions in this size range.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends payment float by 30 days beyond our standard AP cycle, creating additional tracking burden and potential for missed payments.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if subscription is critical-path.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

rejected_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.3A: 'Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials, data, or instructions provided by Customer to Lattice infringe or misappropriate such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A are subject to the indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 and the liability cap and exclusions set forth in Section 8.6, except as provided in Section 8.6(B) for confidentiality breaches.'
rejected_findings.external_comment: The Agreement currently provides only Lattice's IP indemnity. Given that our data and materials are under our control, we propose reciprocal IP indemnity for third-party IP claims arising from our content. This is a standard mutual allocation in SaaS agreements.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Lattice processes and hosts our data as part of the Services. If our data infringes a third party's IP, Lattice has no indemnity protection. Hosting providers are frequently named in IP litigation arising from customer content. Without reciprocal indemnity, Lattice bears uninsured risk for claims we cause.
rejected_findings.position: Mutual IP indemnity: Customer indemnifies Lattice for IP claims arising from Customer Data, Customer-provided materials, and Customer's use of the Subscription Services in violation of law or third-party rights.
rejected_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity limited to Customer Data only, excluding claims arising from Lattice's modification or combination of Customer Data with other data or services.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.3A: 'Customer IP Indemnity. Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data or any materials, data, or instructions provided by Customer to Lattice infringe or misappropriate such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.3A are subject to the indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 and the liability cap and exclusions set forth in Section 8.6, except as provided in Section 8.6(B) for confidentiality breaches.'
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 2.3 states service credits are 'Customer's sole and exclusive remedies' + Section 4.4(b) requires Customer to 'pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach + Section 4.3 gives Customer only 60-day cure window for Lattice breach + Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from four corners
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Customer requires: (1) Exhibit A attached before execution with uptime ≥99.5% and credit cap ≤25% monthly fees; (2) right to terminate for cause without remainder-of-term payment if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months; (3) mutual 30-day cure periods in Section 4.3; (4) clarification that liability cap in Section 8.6 applies to aggregate SLA credit claims.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of missing SLA terms (Exhibit A), sole-remedy language that may or may not be capped, 100% remainder-of-term payment if Customer terminates for non-breach reasons, asymmetric 60-day cure for Lattice vs. 30-day cure for Customer, and no termination-for-repeated-SLA-failure right creates an existential trap: Customer cannot assess SLA adequacy before signing, has no economic exit if SLA proves inadequate (would owe 100% of remaining fees), cannot terminate for repeated SLA failures without paying the full term, and faces a 60-day delay before termination even if Lattice materially breaches SLA commitments. This is not merely the sum of individual findings—it is a structural lock-in that eliminates Customer's remedies for poor service delivery.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Performance-obligations-analyst correctly flagged Exhibit A as blocker (finding 002) and SLA sole-remedy incoherence as major (finding 001). Termination-remedies-analyst correctly flagged cure asymmetry as blocker (finding 001), post-termination payment as major (finding 005), and absence of termination-for-convenience as major (finding 003). However, NO specialist identified the cross-section hazard created by the COMBINATION of these provisions: Customer is signing a contract where (a) performance standards are undefined (Exhibit A missing), (b) the sole remedy for performance failures is unknown and potentially uncapped or potentially illusory, (c) early exit for any reason costs 100% of remaining term with zero service delivery, (d) exit for Lattice breach requires 60-day wait during which Customer continues paying and receiving substandard service, and (e) no termination right exists for repeated SLA failures short of proving material breach. In a high-leverage posture where Customer needs this service, this combination is existential because it eliminates all economic leverage to enforce performance or exit a failing engagement. The structural interaction was missed because each specialist analyzed only their domain.
auditor_findings.position: Before execution: (1) Exhibit A must be attached with commercially reasonable SLA terms. (2) Add termination-for-cause right if SLA credits exceed materiality threshold (e.g., 25% monthly fees for 2 consecutive months) with no remainder-of-term payment obligation. (3) Mutual 30-day cure periods. (4) Clarify liability-cap application to SLA credits. (5) If Customer must terminate for non-breach reasons, cap payment at 50% of remainder or 3 months' fees, whichever is less.
auditor_findings.fallback: (1) Exhibit A provided with 5-day review period and right to negotiate uptime threshold and credit cap. (2) Termination right if SLA credits exceed 50% monthly fees for 2 consecutive months, with 75% remainder-of-term payment. (3) Accept 60/30 cure asymmetry if SLA termination right is granted. (4) Accept liability cap subsumes SLA credits if cap is increased to 2x fees for SLA-related claims.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Signing this Agreement with Exhibit A missing or with no termination right for repeated SLA failures combined with 100% remainder-of-term payment for non-breach termination. This combination eliminates all Customer remedies and creates unquantifiable operational risk in a services engagement where Customer is dependent on continuous availability.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1 governing law). Under Delaware contract law, sole-remedy provisions are enforceable but must be construed with other contractual terms to avoid internal contradictions. Courts will not enforce a remedy structure that is illusory or leaves the non-breaching party without meaningful recourse. The interaction of these provisions may render the sole-remedy language unenforceable, but relying on that outcome in litigation is existential risk for Customer.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Customer requires: (1) Exhibit A attached before execution with uptime ≥99.5% and credit cap ≤25% monthly fees; (2) right to terminate for cause without remainder-of-term payment if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months; (3) mutual 30-day cure periods in Section 4.3; (4) clarification that liability cap in Section 8.6 applies to aggregate SLA credit claims.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) requires 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' + Section 8.6 liability cap at 12 months' fees + finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes data-breach super-cap of '3x fees or insurance limits, whichever is greater' + Section 5.3 requires Security Incident notification within 72 hours + no requirement that Customer be loss payee or that cyber coverage be dedicated standalone policy
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Require: (1) Lattice maintain dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance with $5M limits (not merely E&O with cyber endorsement). (2) Certificate of insurance provided annually showing cyber coverage with no material sublimits or exclusions for first-party data restoration or third-party regulatory defense. (3) Customer named as loss payee on cyber policy for claims arising from Security Incidents. (4) Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 tied explicitly to verified insurance limits, not merely 'whichever is greater' based on undisclosed policy terms.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The proposed data-breach super-cap in risk-allocation-analyst-001 references 'limits of Lattice's cyber liability insurance coverage, whichever is greater,' but Section 9.2(b) does not mandate dedicated cyber insurance—only E&O 'which may include' cyber coverage. If Lattice's E&O policy has cyber sublimits below $5M or excludes certain first-party data-restoration costs, the super-cap reference to 'insurance limits' is ambiguous. Additionally, without loss-payee or additional-insured status on the cyber policy, Customer cannot directly access coverage even if the super-cap applies. This creates an uninsured gap where the super-cap is notionally higher than the general cap but Customer cannot collect because the underlying insurance is inadequate or inaccessible.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Risk-allocation-analyst-001 correctly proposed a data-breach super-cap. Insurance-coverage-analyst-004 correctly flagged ambiguity in Section 9.2(b)'s 'may include cyber' language and requested clarification. However, neither specialist identified the cross-section hazard: if the super-cap is 'insurance limits, whichever is greater' and insurance limits are ambiguous or inaccessible to Customer, the super-cap is illusory. In a material Security Incident (e.g., breach of 100K records triggering $2M in regulatory penalties, notification costs, and business interruption), Customer could face exposure exceeding the 12-month general cap but be unable to collect under the super-cap because (a) Lattice's actual cyber coverage is sublimited to $2M or less, (b) the policy excludes first-party costs, or (c) Customer is not a loss payee and Lattice's carrier denies the claim. This structural interaction was missed because risk-allocation-analyst proposed the super-cap without verifying the insurance mechanics, and insurance-coverage-analyst identified the insurance ambiguity without connecting it to the super-cap enforceability.
auditor_findings.position: Require dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance at $5M with no material sublimits. Customer named as loss payee. Certificate provided annually. Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 set at 3x fees OR verified cyber insurance limits shown on annual certificate, whichever is greater.
auditor_findings.fallback: Accept E&O with cyber endorsement if Lattice provides annual certificate evidencing that cyber sublimits are at least $5M and that first-party data restoration and third-party regulatory defense are covered without material exclusions. Customer named as additional insured (not loss payee) on E&O policy. Super-cap set at 2x fees or insurance limits.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1 governing law). Insurance and indemnity interaction analyzed under general U.S. commercial insurance principles, not Delaware-specific rules.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Require: (1) Lattice maintain dedicated standalone cyber liability insurance with $5M limits (not merely E&O with cyber endorsement). (2) Certificate of insurance provided annually showing cyber coverage with no material sublimits or exclusions for first-party data restoration or third-party regulatory defense. (3) Customer named as loss payee on cyber policy for claims arising from Security Incidents. (4) Data-breach super-cap in Section 8.6 tied explicitly to verified insurance limits, not merely 'whichever is greater' based on undisclosed policy terms.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 10.2 arbitration in San Francisco, California + Section 4.3 asymmetric 60-day cure for Lattice vs. 30-day cure for Customer + Section 4.4(b) Customer pays 100% remainder-of-term fees if Customer terminates for non-breach reasons + no Customer termination-for-convenience right + no defined 'material breach' standard
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Require: (1) Arbitration location mutually agreed or designated by arbitrator as reasonably convenient to both parties (per termination-remedies-analyst-004). (2) Mutual 30-day cure periods (per termination-remedies-analyst-001). (3) Defined material-breach standard (per termination-remedies-analyst-007). (4) Customer termination-for-convenience with reasonable breakage fee (per termination-remedies-analyst-003). Without these, Customer faces: mandatory San Francisco arbitration (adding $50K–$150K cost to any dispute under $200K), 60-day wait to terminate Lattice breach (during which Customer pays and receives substandard service), ambiguity over what constitutes terminating breach (forcing Customer to litigate termination propriety in San Francisco arbitration), and 100% remainder-of-term payment if Customer exits for any reason short of proving material breach in that arbitration.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of vendor-home-state arbitration, asymmetric cure periods, undefined material-breach standard, no termination-for-convenience right, and 100% remainder-of-term payment if Customer exits creates a compounding economic barrier to Customer remedies. If Lattice underperforms (but not to the level of provable material breach), Customer's options are: (1) continue paying for substandard service for the full term, or (2) terminate and pay 100% of remaining fees with zero service, then defend that decision in San Francisco arbitration where Lattice will argue the breach was not material. The arbitration cost (travel, California counsel, time) may exceed the value of the claim, rendering the remedy economically irrational. This is not a bug—it is a feature of the combined terms that structurally favors Lattice.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Termination-remedies-analyst correctly flagged arbitration location as major (finding 004), cure asymmetry as blocker (finding 001), undefined material breach as moderate (finding 007), absence of termination-for-convenience as major (finding 003), and post-termination payment as major (finding 005). However, NO specialist identified the cross-section hazard created by the COMBINATION: the economic irrationality of pursuing any remedy in this structure. Individually, each provision is defensible (arbitration is enforceable, cure periods are negotiable, payment-on-termination is common). But together, they create a remedies regime where Customer's cost to enforce or exit exceeds the value of enforcement or exit for any dispute below $200K–$300K in annual contract value. In a mid-market SaaS deal where annual fees may be $100K–$250K, this is existential to Customer's bargaining position during the term. The structural interaction was missed because termination-remedies-analyst analyzed each provision individually without modeling the economic impact of their combination in a dispute scenario.
auditor_findings.position: Arbitration in mutually agreed location or Customer's home state. Mutual 30-day cure. Defined material-breach standard including repeated SLA failures and payment default. Customer termination-for-convenience with 50% remainder or 3-month cap. This combination restores economic viability of Customer remedies.
auditor_findings.fallback: Arbitration location designated by arbitrator (not San Francisco default). Accept 60/30 cure asymmetry if material-breach standard is defined and includes SLA failures. Customer termination-for-convenience with 75% remainder-of-term payment.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1 governing law). Arbitration situs and enforceability analyzed under Federal Arbitration Act and Delaware choice-of-law rules.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Require: (1) Arbitration location mutually agreed or designated by arbitrator as reasonably convenient to both parties (per termination-remedies-analyst-004). (2) Mutual 30-day cure periods (per termination-remedies-analyst-001). (3) Defined material-breach standard (per termination-remedies-analyst-007). (4) Customer termination-for-convenience with reasonable breakage fee (per termination-remedies-analyst-003). Without these, Customer faces: mandatory San Francisco arbitration (adding $50K–$150K cost to any dispute under $200K), 60-day wait to terminate Lattice breach (during which Customer pays and receives substandard service), ambiguity over what constitutes terminating breach (forcing Customer to litigate termination propriety in San Francisco arbitration), and 100% remainder-of-term payment if Customer exits for any reason short of proving material breach in that arbitration.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-005 proposes to allow Customer to terminate without remainder-of-term payment if Customer terminates 'for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A)'. However, Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from the four corners. The carve-out references a defined term that does not exist in the contract. Either: (1) Exhibit A must be attached and must include a definition of 'Service Level Failure' that triggers the payment-relief carve-out, OR (2) the carve-out in termination-remedies-analyst-005 must be redrafted to reference a different, existing standard (e.g., 'if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months' as proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001).
coherence_findings.external_comment: The proposed edit in termination-remedies-analyst-005 creates a dependency on a missing Exhibit A definition. We need to either attach Exhibit A with the 'Service Level Failure' definition, or tie the payment-relief carve-out to the SLA-based termination right proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001 (which uses a quantified credit-threshold standard). Without this alignment, the carve-out is unenforceable.
