coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If we accept termination-remedies-analyst-005 as drafted but Exhibit A remains missing or does not define 'Service Level Failure,' the payment-relief carve-out is meaningless—Lattice will argue the term is undefined and the general rule (100% remainder payment) applies. This leaves us with no payment relief for SLA-driven termination despite the accepted edit's intent.
coherence_findings.position: Before execution: Exhibit A must define 'Service Level Failure' in a manner consistent with the SLA-based termination right proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001. Alternatively, redraft termination-remedies-analyst-005 to reference the quantified SLA credit threshold from critical-issues-auditor-001 rather than the undefined Exhibit A term.
coherence_findings.fallback: Accept termination-remedies-analyst-005 as drafted only if Exhibit A is attached with 'Service Level Failure' defined as SLA credits exceeding 50% of monthly fees for two consecutive months.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-005 proposes to allow Customer to terminate without remainder-of-term payment if Customer terminates 'for a Lattice Service Level Failure (as defined in Exhibit A)'. However, Exhibit A (SLA document) is missing from the four corners. The carve-out references a defined term that does not exist in the contract. Either: (1) Exhibit A must be attached and must include a definition of 'Service Level Failure' that triggers the payment-relief carve-out, OR (2) the carve-out in termination-remedies-analyst-005 must be redrafted to reference a different, existing standard (e.g., 'if SLA credits exceed 25% of monthly fees for two consecutive months' as proposed in critical-issues-auditor-001).

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.6: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Accepted findings risk-allocation-analyst-001 (data-breach super-cap) and risk-allocation-analyst-002 (gross negligence / willful misconduct carve-out) add new carve-outs (D) and (E) to Section 8.6. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes to clarify that the data-breach super-cap references 'verified insurance limits shown on annual certificate.' These three edits must be reconciled into a single amended Section 8.6 that enumerates all carve-outs (A) through (E) in sequence and ties the insurance-limit language to the certificate-of-insurance requirement in Section 9.2.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Three separate findings propose additions to the liability-cap carve-outs. We need to consolidate these into a single coherent Section 8.6 amendment that: (1) lists all five carve-outs (A-E), (2) defines the data-breach super-cap with reference to verified insurance limits from the annual certificate required under Section 9.2, and (3) does not create ambiguity about the interaction between the super-cap and the insurance-verification requirement.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If we accept all three findings as separate markup items without consolidation, the final redline will have overlapping or conflicting edits to Section 8.6. The super-cap language must explicitly tie to the certificate-of-insurance timing and content requirements, or the 'insurance limits, whichever is greater' standard becomes unverifiable.
coherence_findings.position: Consolidate risk-allocation-analyst-001, risk-allocation-analyst-002, and critical-issues-auditor-002 into a single amended Section 8.6 that lists carve-outs (A) through (E) and ties the data-breach super-cap to the insurance-certificate verification requirement added in critical-issues-auditor-002.
coherence_findings.fallback: Accept the three findings as separate edits but require legal to reconcile them into a single coherent Section 8.6 before presenting the redline to counterparty.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Accepted findings risk-allocation-analyst-001 (data-breach super-cap) and risk-allocation-analyst-002 (gross negligence / willful misconduct carve-out) add new carve-outs (D) and (E) to Section 8.6. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes to clarify that the data-breach super-cap references 'verified insurance limits shown on annual certificate.' These three edits must be reconciled into a single amended Section 8.6 that enumerates all carve-outs (A) through (E) in sequence and ties the insurance-limit language to the certificate-of-insurance requirement in Section 9.2.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 9.1: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement. Lattice is not required to provide advance notice to Customer of, or obtain Customer's consent to, the engagement, replacement, or termination of any subcontractor.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Section 9.1 permits Lattice to engage subcontractors without notice or consent. However, the Profile playbook (playbook.subcontracting.position) requires 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services).' No accepted finding addresses Section 9.1. This is a Profile-contract mismatch that should have been flagged. Propose: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement. Lattice shall provide Customer with prior written notice of the engagement of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services by value. Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable security or compliance grounds within ten (10) business days of notice, and Lattice shall not proceed with such engagement if Customer reasonably withholds consent.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The Profile requires notice and approval for material subcontractors, particularly those handling Customer Data. Section 9.1 currently permits Lattice to engage any subcontractor (including data processors) without notice. This creates data-security and compliance risk, as we have no visibility into the subcontractor supply chain. We propose adding a notice-and-consent mechanism for subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing >25% of Services.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Without notice and approval rights for data-processing subcontractors, we have no visibility into our data-handling supply chain. This creates audit and compliance gaps: we cannot verify that subcontractors meet our data-security standards, and we may face regulatory exposure if a subcontractor's practices violate GDPR, CCPA, or other data-protection frameworks. The Profile identifies this as a priority playbook item.
coherence_findings.position: Add notice and approval requirement for material subcontractors as defined in Profile: notice required + customer approval for subcontractors processing customer data or providing >25% of services. Vendor flowdown of obligations required.
coherence_findings.fallback: Notice required for subcontractors processing Customer Data; approval required only for new data-processing subcontractors added after the Effective Date (grandfather existing subcontractors listed in Order Form).
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement. Lattice shall provide Customer with prior written notice of the engagement of any subcontractor that will process Customer Data or provide more than twenty-five percent (25%) of the Subscription Services by value. Customer may object to any such subcontractor on reasonable security or compliance grounds within ten (10) business days of notice, and Lattice shall not proceed with such engagement if Customer reasonably withholds consent.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 6.1: 'Lattice and its licensors own and shall retain all right, title, and interest in and to the Subscription Services, the Documentation, and any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on any of the foregoing, including all intellectual property rights therein.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Accepted finding protective-provisions-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 6.2A stating 'Customer owns Customer-Specific Deliverables' (defined as reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics created specifically for Customer with no independent utility to Lattice). However, Section 6.1 states that Lattice retains ownership of 'any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on' the Subscription Services. If a Customer-Specific Deliverable (e.g., a custom dashboard) is implemented as a configuration or modification 'to or based on' the Subscription Services platform, Section 6.1's broad retention language could be read to override Section 6.2A's assignment. The two sections must be reconciled with a carve-out in Section 6.1 or a clarification in Section 6.2A that Customer-Specific Deliverables are 'notwithstanding Section 6.1' owned by Customer.
coherence_findings.external_comment: The proposed Customer-Specific Deliverables ownership provision must explicitly override the broad retention language in Section 6.1. Without this clarification, Lattice could argue that a custom report or dashboard built 'based on' the platform is an 'improvement' or 'derivative work' under Section 6.1 and therefore remains Lattice's property despite the Section 6.2A assignment.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Section 6.1 is not explicitly carved out, the Customer-Specific Deliverables assignment in Section 6.2A is ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. In litigation, Lattice would argue the 'improvements and derivative works' retention language in Section 6.1 controls, leaving us without ownership of work product we paid Professional Services fees to create.
coherence_findings.position: Amend Section 6.2A (protective-provisions-analyst-002) to begin: 'Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other work product that Lattice creates specifically for Customer...' to make clear the assignment overrides the general retention language.
coherence_findings.fallback: Add to Section 6.1: 'except as provided in Section 6.2A with respect to Customer-Specific Deliverables.'
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Accepted finding protective-provisions-analyst-002 proposes adding new Section 6.2A stating 'Customer owns Customer-Specific Deliverables' (defined as reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics created specifically for Customer with no independent utility to Lattice). However, Section 6.1 states that Lattice retains ownership of 'any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on' the Subscription Services. If a Customer-Specific Deliverable (e.g., a custom dashboard) is implemented as a configuration or modification 'to or based on' the Subscription Services platform, Section 6.1's broad retention language could be read to override Section 6.2A's assignment. The two sections must be reconciled with a carve-out in Section 6.1 or a clarification in Section 6.2A that Customer-Specific Deliverables are 'notwithstanding Section 6.1' owned by Customer.
